None of the search results directly provide your local network IP or the MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) of your connection. They are all focused on finding your public IP address and providing geolocation information.

To find your local network IP, you'll need to use a different approach. On your Manjaro Linux system, you can use the `ip` command to find your local IP address:

```bash
ip addr show
```

This will display a list of network interfaces, including their IP addresses. Look for the interface that corresponds to your local network connection (e.g., `wlan0` for Wi-Fi or `eth0` for Ethernet).

As for the MTU of your connection, you can use the `ip link` command to find it:

```bash
ip link show
```

This will display information about your network interfaces, including their MTU values. Look for the interface that corresponds to your local network connection.

Alternatively, you can use the `ip` command with the `link` and `show` options to find the MTU of a specific interface:

```bash
ip link show dev <interface_name> | grep mtu
```

Replace `<interface_name>` with the actual name of your network interface (e.g., `wlan0` or `eth0`).

To create 1000 folders inside 1 folder named "test folder" in Downloads, you can use the command line. Assuming you're on Linux (Manjaro), navigate to the Downloads directory and create the "test folder". Then, use a loop to create 1000 folders inside it.

```bash
cd ~/Downloads
mkdir test\ folder
for i in {1..1000}; do mkdir "test folder/$i"; done
```

This will create 1000 folders named "1" to "1000" inside the "test folder". 

Alternatively, you can use a single command:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/Downloads/test\ folder/{1..1000}
```
